posts.hook: You've been taking screen breaks wrong. The 20-20-20 rule has a version that actually works — and almost no one does it correctly.
posts.caption:
Most people do the 20-20-20 rule like this:

❌ Look at the wall 20 feet away
❌ Glance at a window briefly
❌ Keep screen open in peripheral vision

None of that works.

Your ciliary muscle never fully releases.

The correct version:
✅ Every 20 minutes
✅ Look OUTSIDE — real depth, not flat surface
✅ 20 full seconds — blink 5× deliberately

That's it. That's the whole exercise.

Guided timer at axeo.vision/exercise

#2020rule #EyeStrain #ScreenTime #Axeo #HealthTok #EyeHealth #DigitalEyeStrain

posts.hook: Why your eyes hurt after 2 hours on a screen — the ciliary muscle no one told you about.
posts.caption:
Every time you look at your phone, a muscle inside your eye contracts.

The ciliary muscle.

It squeezes a tiny lens — the crystalline lens — to change focus.

At arm's length: muscle contracted
At 20 feet: muscle relaxed

After hours of near work:
- The muscle fatigues
- It can cramp and refuse to relax (ciliary spasm)
- Your eyes ache, water, blur

This is digital eye strain. Not a disease. A muscle overuse problem.

The 20-20-20 rule gives the ciliary muscle a mandatory rest.

Every 20 minutes → 20 feet → 20 seconds.

Guided version with a built-in timer: axeo.vision/exercise

#DigitalEyeStrain #CiliaryMuscle #EyeHealth #Axeo #AxeoVision #20-20-20 #EyeScience

posts.hook: The 20-20-20 rule was designed by ophthalmologists — here's what it actually does to your eye muscles.
posts.caption:
20-20-20 isn't a wellness trend.

It's biomechanics.

Your crystalline lens is focused by the ciliary muscle.

Near work keeps this muscle contracted — indefinitely.

No other muscle in your body is asked to hold one position for 6–8 hours straight.

After sustained contraction:
→ Ciliary spasm — muscle locks up
→ Pseudomyopia — temporary blurring of distant vision
→ Headache, burning, watering eyes

The 20-20-20 rule forces a mandatory relaxation.

Every 20 minutes = a rest set for your eye muscles.

Free guided timer: axeo.vision/exercise

#20-20-20 #EyeHealth #DigitalEyeStrain #EyeExercise #Axeo #CiliaryMuscle #VisionScience

posts.hook: The 20-20-20 rule is one of the most recommended pieces of eye health advice. Almost everyone does it wrong. A thread:
posts.caption:
1/ The 20-20-20 rule: every 20 minutes of screen time, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds. Simple. But there are two common mistakes that make it useless.

2/ Mistake 1: Looking at a wall or flat surface. A wall 20 feet away provides no depth cue. Your ciliary muscle adjusts based on depth — not distance on a flat plane. You need to look at something with actual depth: outdoors, or across a large room with real objects.

3/ Mistake 2: Not blinking. During focused screen use, blink rate drops from 15–20x/min to 3–5x/min. Dry eyes compound fatigue. During your 20-second break, blink deliberately — 5 times, fully. This recoats the tear film.

4/ The physiology: your ciliary muscle is a smooth muscle that contracts to focus on near objects. Unlike skeletal muscle, it has no built-in fatigue signal. It will hold indefinitely — and eventually spasm. The 20-20-20 rule forces relaxation before spasm onset.

5/ In practice: set a 20-minute browser tab timer, stand up, look out a window, blink 5 times, sit back down. Total interruption: 35–40 seconds. Research shows consistent practitioners report significantly less end-of-day eye fatigue.

6/ Axeo has a guided 20-20-20 timer built into the exercise page. Free, browser-based, no download: axeo.vision/exercise

Share if this corrected something you've been doing.

posts.hook: The productivity cost of digital eye strain — and a 35-second intervention that fixes it.
posts.caption:
Knowledge workers stare at screens for 6–9 hours a day.

At the end of that day, many report:
→ Headaches
→ Blurred near or distance vision
→ Burning or watering eyes
→ Difficulty concentrating in the final hours

This is not a motivation problem. It's a muscle problem.

The ciliary muscle — which controls focus — is held in near-constant contraction during screen work. Unlike skeletal muscles, it has no built-in fatigue signaling that interrupts performance. It just cramps.

The fix is well-established and free: the 20-20-20 rule.
Every 20 minutes → look 20 feet away → 20 full seconds → blink 5 times.

Total time cost: 35–40 seconds per cycle.
Expected productivity impact: cognitive fatigue typically begins accelerating in the final 2 hours of a screen-heavy workday. Consistent 20-20-20 practitioners often report extending productive focus time by 45–60 minutes.

For teams:
- Set shared Slack reminders every 20 minutes
- Encourage standing and looking out windows (not at walls)
- Track before/after eye strain scores with free tools like axeo.vision

The easiest wellness intervention most teams haven't tried.

#EmployeeWellbeing #WorkplaceHealth #EyeHealth #RemoteWork #ProductivityTips #EyeStrain
